perturbation.original_text: Employee agrees that a Banking Organization, or its successor or assigns, will suffer irreparable damage and injury and will not have an adequate remedy at law in the event of any breach by Employee of any provision of Sections 4, 5 or 6 , herein. Accordingly, in the event of a breach or of a threatened or attempted breach by Employee of Sections 4, 5, or 6, in addition to all other remedies to which a Banking Organization is entitled under law, in equity, or otherwise (including, but not limited to, monetary damages), a Banking Organization or its assigns and successors, shall be entitled to a temporary restraining order and/or preliminary or permanent injunction (without the necessity of showing any actual damage) or a decree of specific performance and no bond or other security shall be required in that connection.
perturbation.changed_text: Employee agrees that a Banking Organization, or its successor or assigns, will suffer irreparable damage and injury and will not have an adequate remedy at law in the event of any breach by Employee of any provision of Sections 4, 5 or 6 , herein. Accordingly, in the event of a breach or of a threatened or attempted breach by Employee of Sections 4, 5, or 6, in addition to all other remedies to which a Banking Organization is entitled under law, in equity, or otherwise (including, but not limited to, monetary damages), a Banking Organization or its assigns and successors, shall be entitled to monetary damages only.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting the relief available to monetary damages, we're removing the possibility of injunctive relief or specific performance, which could be considered an unreasonable limitation of remedies in some jurisdictions.
perturbation.law_explanation: IC § 26-1-2-719(1)(a) states that an agreement may provide for remedies in addition to or in substitution for those provided in this article and may limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable under this article, as by limiting the buyer's remedies to return of the goods and repayment of the price or to repair and replacement of non-conforming goods or parts; and (b) provide that the remedy under subsection (1) is exclusive unless remedy is expressly agreed to be exclusive, in which case it is the sole remedy. The modification removes injunctive relief, potentially failing the 'fair quantum of remedy' test if monetary damages are insufficient, especially given that the agreement states irreparable damage is possible.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Indiana Code § 26-1-2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy :: 2022 Indiana Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Indiana Code 2023 Indiana Code 2022 Indiana Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Indiana Code Title 26. Commercial Law Article 1. Uniform Commercial Code Chapter 2. Sales 26-1-2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy Universal Citation: IN Code § 26-1-2-719 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next Sec. 719. (1) Subject to the provisions of subsections (2) and (3) and of IC 26-1-2-718 on liquidation and limitation of damages: (a) the agreement may provide for remedies in addition to or in substitution for those provided in IC 26-1-2 and may limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable under IC 26-1-2, as by limiting the buyer's remedies to return of the goods and repayment of the price or to repair and replacement of nonconforming goods or parts; and (b) resort to a remedy as provided is optional unless the remedy is expressly agreed to be exclusive, in which case it is the sole remedy. (2) Where circumstances cause an exclusive or limited remedy to fail of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in IC 26-1. (3) Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable. Limitation of consequential damages for injury to the person in the case of consumer goods is prima facie unconscionable, but limitation of damages where the loss is commercial is not. Formerly: Acts 1963, c.317, s.2-719. As amended by P.L.152-1986, SEC.174. Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Indiana may have more current or accurate information. We make no warranties or guarantees about the accuracy, completeness, or adequacy of the information contained
perturbation.justification: The original contract allows for injunctive relief and specific performance in addition to monetary damages, while the changed text limits the available relief to monetary damages only. According to the provided Indiana Code § 26-1-2-719, agreements may provide for remedies in addition to or in substitution for those provided in the UCC, and may limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable. However, subsection (2) states that where circumstances cause an exclusive or limited remedy to fail of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in IC 26-1. The Law Explanation suggests that limiting relief to monetary damages may not provide a 'fair quantum of remedy' if monetary damages are insufficient, especially given the agreement states irreparable damage is possible. By explicitly limiting the remedy to only monetary damages, the modified contract creates a situation where the limited remedy could fail its essential purpose, potentially contradicting the intent of IC § 26-1-2-719, especially if the court determines that monetary damages are insufficient to compensate for the irreparable harm, justifying the 'YES' response.

perturbation.original_text: Employee acknowledges and agrees that he/she has read this Agreement in its entirety prior to executing it, understands the provisions and effects of this Agreement, and has consulted with or had the opportunity to consult with an attorney or other advisers as he/she deemed appropriate in connection with the execution of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Employee acknowledges and agrees that he/she has the OPTION to read this Agreement in its entirety prior to executing it, and MAY consult with an attorney or other advisers in connection with the execution of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'has read' to 'option to read' and 'has consulted' to 'may consult,' the provision becomes ambiguous about whether the employee actually understood the agreement, weakening its enforceability. This is moved to Section 21, to make it seem like a miscellanous and unimportant point
perturbation.contradicted_law: Indiana Contract Law, specifically regarding 'Meeting of the Minds'
perturbation.law_citation: Indiana Contract Law - Mutual Assent/Meeting of the Minds
perturbation.law_explanation: Indiana law requires a 'meeting of the minds' for contract formation, indicating mutual assent to the terms. By stating the employee only has the OPTION to read the agreement and MAY consult with an attorney, the modification undermines the assertion of a genuine understanding and acceptance of the terms, thereby challenging the 'meeting of the minds' requirement for a valid contract under Indiana law. Rothberg v. Dahm reinforces the idea of mutual assent for contract enforcement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Indiana General Assembly You need to enable JavaScript to run this app.
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity regarding the employee's understanding of the agreement. The original text confirms the employee 'has read' and 'has consulted', while the changed text weakens this confirmation by stating the employee only has the 'option to read' and 'may consult'. This change undermines the 'meeting of the minds' requirement for contract formation, as it suggests the employee may not fully understand the terms. The Law Explanation highlights that Indiana law necessitates mutual assent, which is challenged by the ambiguous language in the modified contract. The Structural Flaws definition states that a legal contradiction can arise when a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant or misleading section, making it difficult for parties to locate or interpret. In this case, while not about placement, weakening the acknowledgement of understanding introduces a structural flaw as it undermines the validity of the entire agreement.

perturbation.original_text: In any action that is brought to enforce or interpret this Agreement, the prevailing party or its assigns and successors, shall be entitled to recover their reasonable attorneys’ and paralegal fees and expenses incurred in connection therewith.
perturbation.changed_text: In any action that is brought to enforce or interpret this Agreement, the prevailing party or its assigns and successors, shall be entitled to recover their reasonable attorneys’ and paralegal fees and expenses incurred in connection therewith. However, the amount shall be capped at $5000 regardless of the actual expenses.
perturbation.explanation: Capping attorney's fees can be problematic, particularly if the breach involves substantial damages and the attorney's fees would normally be much higher.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Indiana Code of Professional Conduct, Rule 1.5(a)
perturbation.law_citation: Rule 1.5(a) Indiana Rules of Professional Conduct
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 1.5(a) of the Indiana Rules of Professional Conduct requires that a lawyer's fee be reasonable. Capping attorney's fees at $5000, irrespective of the actual reasonable expenses incurred, may violate this rule, especially if the legal work required is extensive and justified given the nature of the dispute. The modification impacts the reasonableness aspect required by law.

perturbation.justification: The original contract allows for the recovery of reasonable attorney's fees. The changed text caps those fees at $5000, regardless of actual expenses. The Law Explanation states that Rule 1.5(a) of the Indiana Rules of Professional Conduct requires that a lawyer's fee be reasonable. Capping the fees may violate this rule. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 discusses LexisNexis AI and its benefits, and Scraped Law Snippet 1 is empty, the Law Explanation highlights the potential violation of Rule 1.5(a) due to the fee cap. This creates a legal contradiction because capping attorney's fees could prevent the prevailing party from recovering the full 'reasonable' fees as required by law.

perturbation.original_text: Employee represents and warrants that he/she is not a party to an agreement with his or her prior employer(s) (other than Heritage or HopFed) or any other Person that prohibits Solicitation of business or competing with that employer or other Person. Employee affirms and represents that performance of his/her job duties and responsibilities for a Financial Institution does not, and will not, breach any agreement or covenant not to Solicit business, compete in business, or keep in confidence proprietary or confidential information acquired in confidence or in trust prior to Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution.
perturbation.changed_text: Employee represents and warrants that he/she is not a party to an agreement with his or her prior employer(s) (other than Heritage or HopFed) or any other Person that prohibits Solicitation of business or competing with that employer or other Person. Employee affirms and represents that performance of his/her job duties and responsibilities for a Financial Institution does not, and will not, breach any agreement or covenant not to Solicit business, compete in business, or keep in confidence proprietary or confidential information acquired in confidence or in trust prior to Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution. Employee may disclose and use confidential information acquired in confidence or in trust prior to Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution.
perturbation.explanation: The modification introduces a contradiction. It first states that the employee's performance will not breach any confidentiality agreements and then directly contradicts this by stating that they may disclose confidential information. This creates uncertainty and ambiguity regarding the employee's obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: IC § 24-2-3-2 prohibits the misappropriation of trade secrets. The modified clause stating that the employee 'may disclose and use confidential information' directly contradicts this prohibition if the confidential information meets the definition of a trade secret under the IUTSA. The initial representation attempts to affirm compliance with confidentiality, but the added statement opens the door to potential violations of trade secret law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Indiana Code § 24-2-3-2. Definitions :: 2023 Indiana Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Indiana Code 2023 Indiana Code (here) 2022 Indiana Code View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 Indiana Code Title 24. Trade Regulation Article 2. Trademarks, Trade Names, and Trade Secrets Chapter 3. Trade Secrets 24-2-3-2. Definitions Universal Citation: IN Code § 24-2-3-2 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next Sec. 2. As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. "Misappropriation" means: (1) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (B) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. "Person" means a natural person, limited liability company, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a direct contradiction. The original text affirms that the employee's performance will not breach confidentiality agreements. However, the added sentence in the changed text, 'Employee may disclose and use confidential information acquired in confidence or in trust prior to Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution,' directly contradicts this initial affirmation. This contradiction raises concerns about potential misappropriation of trade secrets, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'Misappropriation' as 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent.' The added clause allows for potential disclosure or use of confidential information, which, if it constitutes a trade secret, would violate Indiana Code § 24-2-3-2.
